business_goals: Achieve Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) compliance
Establish an Open Software Architecture design with layered interfaces
Create an integration model for legacy code, new code, and COTS products
technical_constraints: Limited access to electronic CASE tool data
Short timeline and limited resources for assessment (necessitating a hybrid ATAM)

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Standard contract documents (CONOPS, SRS, SSDD) were available in paper/electronic document format. However, native electronic design data (Rational Rose, Requisite Pro) was not accessible, making the hierarchical architecture difficult to browse.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: What are the driving architectural constraints, and where are they documented?
How do components communicate and synchronize?
What are the styles of architectural approaches?

hardware_view_description: System must support portability across Linux and Windows NT platforms
Low-level I/O interface layer defined in N-tier architecture to handle platform differences
runtime_constraints: System has a statically set limit for the number of simultaneous connections (links)
Legacy code imposes single-threaded control models on many components

questions: What is the process/thread model of the architecture?
What are the system states and modes?
What variability points are included in the architecture?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: Introduction of a Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) for data sharing and enterprise services.
System needs to support a number of links beyond the currently anticipated statically set limit.
Integration of Web Service (WS) based modules across system components.
Replacement of a current component with a known external interface.

architectural_approaches.description: The system utilizes an N-tier structure with a dedicated low-level I/O interface layer to separate platform-specific hardware logic from application-level logic.

addressed_attributes.rationale: The low-level I/O layer abstracts differences between Linux and Windows NT, allowing higher layers to remain platform-agnostic.
Logical separation of concerns into tiers allows for the independent evolution of the user interface, business logic, and data access components.

architectural_approaches.approach_name: Open Software Architecture (OSA) with Standardized Interfaces
architectural_approaches.description: A design approach focused on layered interfaces to facilitate the integration of new code, legacy code, and COTS products while meeting Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) standards.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Standardized, layered interfaces are specifically designed to meet NR-KPP requirements for communicating with external systems and COTS software.
The open architecture allows for the replacement or upgrade of individual components (legacy or COTS) without redesigning the entire system.

architectural_approaches.description: Encapsulation of legacy code segments, which are constrained by single-threaded control models, to integrate them into the broader multi-tiered environment.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Wrapping legacy code allows new system features to interact with old logic through modernized, compatible interfaces.
Isolating single-threaded legacy control models within wrappers prevents legacy-specific execution constraints from causing failures in the concurrent parts of the N-tier system.

children.scenario_description: Migrate the system from Linux to Windows NT using the low-level I/O interface layer.
Integrate a new COTS product via standardized OSA interfaces to meet NR-KPP standards.
Isolate a failure in a single-threaded legacy component within its wrapper to prevent system-wide deadlock.
Replace the User Interface tier with a new framework without modifying the business logic tier.

scenarios.scenario_text: Migrate the system from Linux to Windows NT using the low-level I/O interface layer while maintaining full functional parity.
Integrate a new external COTS product via standardized OSA interfaces to meet Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) standards without modifying core business logic.
Isolate a runtime failure within a single-threaded legacy code component using its wrapper to ensure the multi-threaded N-tier system remains responsive.
Replace the existing User Interface tier with a modern web-based framework without necessitating any changes to the underlying business logic tier.

scenario_reference.text: Migrate the system from Linux to Windows NT using the low-level I/O interface layer while maintaining full functional parity.

analysis_records.stimulus: Porting request from Linux to Windows NT
analysis_records.response_measure: Minimal code changes in upper tiers; 100% functional parity
analysis_records.reasoning: By abstracting OS-specific calls into a dedicated layer, upper tiers remain agnostic of the underlying kernel. However, this creates a dependency on the completeness of the abstraction layer mapping.

risks.rationale: Differences in asynchronous I/O models between Linux and Windows NT may not be fully captured by the abstraction layer, leading to runtime errors.

non_risks.rationale: The N-tier separation ensures that business logic is completely decoupled from hardware-specific logic.

scenario_reference.text: Integrate a new external COTS product via standardized OSA interfaces to meet Net-Ready Key Performance Parameter (NR-KPP) standards without modifying core business logic.

analysis_records.stimulus: Integration of a new COTS component
analysis_records.reasoning: Standardized interfaces facilitate plug-and-play capability for external components, provided the components adhere to the interface specifications.

risks.rationale: COTS products often use proprietary data formats that may require complex translation logic to fit OSA standards.

non_risks.rationale: OSA allows for the replacement of COTS products without rewriting the entire system.

scenario_reference.text: Isolate a runtime failure within a single-threaded legacy code component using its wrapper to ensure the multi-threaded N-tier system remains responsive.

analysis_records.reasoning: The wrapper acts as a protective boundary, managing the synchronization between the multi-threaded environment and the single-threaded legacy execution.

risks.rationale: If the wrapper's internal locking mechanism is not robust, a failure in the legacy code could hold a lock indefinitely, blocking the main system.

non_risks.rationale: The wrapper allows the reuse of validated legacy logic without rewriting it for multi-threading.

scenario_reference.text: Replace the existing User Interface tier with a modern web-based framework without necessitating any changes to the underlying business logic tier.

analysis_records.response_measure: Zero changes to the Business Logic tier
architectural_decisions: Separation of UI and Business Logic
analysis_records.reasoning: The layered approach isolates the presentation logic from the domain logic, allowing the UI to evolve independently.

risks.rationale: If the business logic tier expects specific UI-related data structures, replacing the UI will force changes in the BL tier.

non_risks.rationale: Layering allows the UI and BL tiers to be scaled independently on different hardware if needed.
